statement.markdown: Implement a function `sortArray` that sorts a dynamic array of integers in-place using the QuickSort algorithm. The function should take two parameters: `arr` (the input array) and `length` (the number of elements in the array). The function should modify the original array and return nothing. Note that the input array may contain duplicate elements and the function should be able to handle such cases.
statement.inputFormat: The input will be a dynamic array of integers and a number representing the length of the array.
statement.outputFormat: The function should not return anything, but the array should be sorted in-place.
statement.notes: Make sure to handle the edge cases where the length of the array is 0 or 1.

examples.explanation: The input array is sorted in-place using the QuickSort algorithm.

hints.text: Choose a good pivot element for the QuickSort algorithm.
Make sure to handle the edge cases where the length of the array is 0 or 1.

editorial.title: Editorial for Fix the Dynamic Array Sorter
editorial.intuition: The QuickSort algorithm is a divide-and-conquer algorithm that works by selecting a 'pivot' element from the array and partitioning the other elements into two sub-arrays, according to whether they are less than or greater than the pivot.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach to sorting the array would be to use a simple sorting algorithm like BubbleSort or SelectionSort.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution would be to use a hybrid sorting algorithm that combines the benefits of both QuickSort and InsertionSort.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to use a well-implemented QuickSort algorithm that handles the edge cases correctly.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness for the QuickSort algorithm involves showing that the partitions are correctly formed and that the final sorted array is the correct one.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the QuickSort algorithm would involve selecting a pivot element, partitioning the array, and then recursively sorting the sub-arrays.
